stem: Let \( f(x) = ([[p]])x + ([[q]]) \). Find the value \( c \) in \( ([[a]], [[b]]) \) guaranteed by the Mean Value Theorem for Integrals, i.e. the number \( c \) for which \( f(c) \) equals the average value of \( f \) on \( [[[a]], [[b]]] \).
distractor_rationales: This reports the average value \( \bar f \) itself, not the point \( c \) where \( f(c) = \bar f \).
This gives the left endpoint of the interval rather than the guaranteed interior point.
This gives the right endpoint of the interval rather than the guaranteed interior point.
This forgets to halve the sum of the endpoints when locating the midpoint.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

stem: Let \( f(x) = x^{2} \) on \( [[[a]], [[b]]] \), where \( 0 < [[a]] < [[b]] \). The Mean Value Theorem for Integrals guarantees a \( c \) in \( ([[a]], [[b]]) \) with \( f(c) \) equal to the average value of \( f \) on \( [[[a]], [[b]]] \). Find this \( c \) (take the positive root, since \( c \) must lie in \( ([[a]], [[b]]) \)).
solution: The average value of \( f(x)=x^{2} \) on \( [[[a]], [[b]]] \) is \( \bar f = \frac{1}{[[b]]-[[a]]}\int_{[[a]]}^{[[b]]} t^{2}\, dt = \frac{[[a]]^{2}+[[a]]\cdot[[b]]+[[b]]^{2}}{3} \). Since \( f \) is increasing on this positive interval, the unique \( c \) with \( c^{2}=\bar f \) and \( c \in ([[a]],[[b]]) \) is \( c = \sqrt{\bar f} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses the midpoint of the interval, which is only the MVT point for a LINEAR function, not for \( x^{2} \).
This reports the average value \( \bar f \) itself instead of solving \( c^{2}=\bar f \) for \( c \).
This averages the SQUARES of the endpoints directly instead of averaging \( x^{2} \) over the whole interval.
This takes the square root of the average of the endpoints' squares rather than the true average value \( \frac{a^2+ab+b^2}{3} \).
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

stem: A thermometer records the temperature at time \( t \) hours as \( T(t) = ([[m]])t + ([[T0]]) \) degrees. By the Mean Value Theorem for Integrals, there is a time \( c \) between hour [[t1]] and hour [[t2]] at which the instantaneous temperature \( T(c) \) equals the AVERAGE temperature over that span. Find \( c \).
solution: The average temperature over \( [[[t1]],[[t2]]] \) is \( \bar T = \frac{1}{[[t2]]-[[t1]]}\int_{[[t1]]}^{[[t2]]} T = ([[m]])\frac{[[t1]]+[[t2]]}{2}+([[T0]]) \). Since \( T \) is linear, the guaranteed time is the midpoint \( c=\frac{[[t1]]+[[t2]]}{2} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This reports the average temperature itself, not the TIME at which it occurs.
This gives the start of the time span rather than the guaranteed interior time.
This gives the end of the time span rather than the guaranteed interior time.
This forgets to halve the sum of the two times when locating the midpoint.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

stem: Let \( f(x) = ([[p]])x + ([[q]]) \). On the interval \( ([[a]], b) \), the Mean Value Theorem for Integrals is satisfied by \( c = [[c]] \), i.e. \( f([[c]]) \) equals the average value of \( f \) on \( [[[a]], b] \). Find \( b \) (given \( b > [[a]] \)).
solution: Since \( f \) is linear, the guaranteed point is always the midpoint of the interval: \( [[c]] = \frac{[[a]]+b}{2} \). Solving for \( b \) gives \( b = 2([[c]]) - ([[a]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This computes the interval's width instead of solving the midpoint equation for \( b \).
This adds instead of solving \( c=(a+b)/2 \) for \( b \).
This drops the minus sign when isolating \( b \) from the midpoint equation.
This halves \( c \) before doubling, rather than doubling \( c \) first.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

stem: Let \( f(x) = |x| \) on \( [-[[w]], [[w]]] \). This \( f \) is continuous but not differentiable at \( x=0 \); the Mean Value Theorem for Integrals still applies since it only requires continuity. Which of the following is A value of \( c \) in \( (-[[w]], [[w]]) \) at which \( f(c) \) equals the average value of \( f \) on \( [-[[w]], [[w]]] \)?
solution: The average value of \( |x| \) on \( [-[[w]],[[w]]] \) is \( \bar f = \frac{1}{2\cdot[[w]]}\int_{-[[w]]}^{[[w]]} |x|\,dx = \frac{[[w]]}{2} \). Solving \( |c| = [[w]]/2 \) gives TWO guaranteed points, \( c = \pm [[w]]/2 \); \( [[answer]] \) is one of them.
distractor_rationales: This is an endpoint of the interval, not an interior point where \( f \) meets its average.
This is the other endpoint of the interval, not an interior point where \( f \) meets its average.
This uses a quarter of \( [[w]] \) instead of the correct average value \( [[w]]/2 \).
This uses the corner point \( x=0 \), where \( f(0)=0 \ne \bar f \) (unless \( [[w]]=0 \)).
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This doubles the interval half-width instead of halving it.
